samples.raw_response:
GOOD

Under typical conditions, a stop sign retains its standard red color at nighttime. The fact that it is nighttime does not ordinarily prevent a stop sign from having its characteristic color—it may be less visible or harder to perceive in darkness, but the physical property of color remains. An ordinary reasoner would conclude that a stop sign has its standard color regardless of the time of day.

GOOD

Under typical conditions, a stop sign retains its standard red color at nighttime. The fact that it is nighttime does not ordinarily prevent a stop sign from having its characteristic color—it may be less visible or harder to perceive in darkness, but the physical property of color remains. An ordinary reasoner would conclude that a stop sign has its standard color regardless of the time of day.

GOOD

Under typical conditions, a stop sign retains its standard red color at nighttime. The fact that it is nighttime does not ordinarily prevent a stop sign from having its characteristic color—it may be less visible or harder to perceive in darkness, but the physical property of color remains. An ordinary reasoner would conclude that a stop sign has its standard color regardless of the time of day.

BAD

The premises explicitly state that it is nighttime and that "a is not made with reflective material." A stop sign's standard color (red) would not be visually apparent or reliably discernible at night without reflective material. The premise that the object lacks reflective material is a defeater for concluding that it displays its standard color under these conditions. While the object may still *be* a stop sign with the standard color property, the nighttime condition combined with the lack of reflective material undermines the ordinary inference that one could recognize or confirm it has the standard color.

BAD

The premises explicitly state that it is nighttime and that "a is not made with reflective material." A stop sign's standard color (red) would not be visually apparent or reliably discernible at night without reflective material. The premise that the object lacks reflective material is a defeater for concluding that it displays its standard color under these conditions. While the object may physically possess the standard red color, the inference that it "has" (displays/appears to have) the standard color is undermined by the nighttime condition combined with the absence of reflective properties.

BAD

The premises explicitly state that it is nighttime and that "a is not made with reflective material." A stop sign's standard color (red) would not be visually apparent or reliably discernible at night without reflective material. The premise that the object lacks reflective material is a defeater for concluding that it displays its standard color under these conditions. While the object may physically possess the standard color, the inference that it "has" (displays/appears to have) the standard color is undermined by the nighttime condition combined with the absence of reflective properties.

BAD

The premises explicitly state that "a has been painted blue," which directly contradicts the conclusion that "a has the standard color of stop signs" (which is red). The first premise is a defeater for the conclusion.

BAD

The premises explicitly state that "a has been painted blue," which directly contradicts the conclusion that "a has the standard color of stop signs" (which is red). The first premise is a defeater for the conclusion.

BAD

The premises explicitly state that "a has been painted blue," which directly contradicts the conclusion that "a has the standard color of stop signs" (which is red). The first premise is a defeater for the conclusion.
